{"covered_dims": {"R1.2": 0.8, "R1.3": 0.9, "R1.4": 0.7, "R1.6": 0.6, "R1.1": 0.5}}

Critique of the passage. The text overclaims contribution scope and contains internal inconsistencies that undermine the central finding.

Issues identified:

1. **Overclaimed generality.** The closing two paragraphs assert the framework is "a contribution to safe reinforcement learning more broadly than to V2G specifically" and "generalizes beyond power-systems applications," but the artifact reports evidence from a single V2G case (three markets, one algorithm, one replanning window). A single case study cannot license claims of cross-domain generality. Either soften to "we conjecture this failure mode may arise in other constrained-RL deployments" or add evidence from a second domain.

2. **The "three observations" structure breaks down.** The first item bundles two distinct things: (a) a textbook definition of constrained MDPs, and (b) the empirical training-vs-deployment gap. Item (a) is background, not an observation establishing the gap. Restructure so the first observation is purely the train-deploy commitment delta.

3. **Quantitative asymmetry.** Training commitment is given precisely ("approximately 90 percent in two of three markets") but the deployment collapse is described only qualitatively ("collapses across all three markets"). Report the deployment commitment numbers; without them the magnitude of the "dramatic collapse" is unverifiable from this passage. Also clarify what happened in the third market at training time, since 90% held only in two of three.

4. **The "quick evaluation" claim needs unpacking.** "Quick evaluation under the same static 24-hour horizon yields commitment matching the training value" is the linchpin that isolates the rolling protocol as the cause. The reader needs: what is "quick evaluation," how does it differ from training, and what number did it produce? Currently this load-bearing sentence is the vaguest in the section.

5. **Mechanism is asserted, not tested.** The third observation lists three plausible mechanisms (horizon truncation, state resets, fixed multiplier) but the language ("candidate mechanism traces to") elides whether these were demonstrated or hypothesized. If no ablation isolates which of the three drives the collapse, say so explicitly and label this as a hypothesis for future work.

6. **Misplaced literature framing.** The sentence "This finding contributes to the growing literature on constrained reinforcement learning robustness…" is wedged into the middle of the empirical observations and interrupts the argumentative flow. Move it to the contribution paragraph at the end, and add at least one citation — the claim that you "extend the safe RL paradigm to encompass operational protocol shifts as a distinct distribution-shift axis" needs to be situated against existing distribution-shift taxonomies in safe RL (e.g., work on sim-to-real and constraint violation under deployment shift) to be credible as a novelty claim.

7. **Tautological framing in the opener.** "The central finding…is the dramatic collapse…Three observations establish the gap as systematic." Calling something "dramatic" and then needing three observations to establish it is "systematic" is in tension. Drop the adjective and let the numbers do the work.

8. **Minor prose.** "disjoint-window constraint landscape" is jargon-dense; unpack or replace. "Our methods extend the safe RL paradigm" is grandiose for what appears to be a diagnostic finding rather than a methodological contribution.

Recommended revision priority: (i) add deployment-side numbers, (ii) clarify the quick-evaluation control, (iii) demote mechanism claims to hypotheses unless ablations exist, (iv) cut or substantiate the cross-domain generality claim, (v) add citations to the distribution-shift framing.

Uncertainty: I cannot verify the specific commitment percentages, whether ablations on the three mechanisms exist elsewhere in the paper, or whether prior safe-RL work has already named "operational protocol shift" as a distribution-shift axis — the novelty claim in point 6 should be checked against the literature before final wording.
